pick.rationale: Arizona's historically elite defense—ranked #1 in KenPom (AdjD 89.7) and 3rd in Torvik (91.1)—is the decisive factor in this matchup, as it should significantly blunt Arkansas's 5th-ranked offense (AdjO 127.6), which is the Razorbacks' primary weapon. The roughly 12-point adjusted efficiency margin gap (+37.56 vs +25.43) is enormous for a 1-vs-4 Sweet 16 matchup, and while Arkansas enters with strong momentum as SEC Tournament champions under Calipari, their mediocre defense (AdjD 102.1, 50th nationally) creates a critical vulnerability that Arizona's 7th-ranked offense will exploit. With Koa Peat fully healthy after his February leg injury and Arizona riding a 31-2 record as one of the most dominant teams in the country, the Wildcats' two-way superiority should carry them comfortably into the Elite Eight.

reasoningStep.title: Arizona vs Arkansas – West Sweet 16 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Arizona's elite two-way profile creates a massive quality gap over Arkansas despite the Razorbacks' top-5 offense and SEC Tournament championship momentum.
evidence: Arizona ranks 3rd nationally in both KenPom (AdjEM +37.56) and Torvik with the nation's best defense (AdjD 89.7) and 7th-ranked offense (AdjO 127.2)
Arkansas ranks 17th in KenPom (AdjEM +25.43) with the 5th-ranked offense (AdjO 127.6) but only a 50th-ranked defense (AdjD 102.1)
The ~12-point efficiency margin gap per 100 possessions is one of the largest in any Sweet 16 matchup
Koa Peat returned fully healthy from his February leg injury on March 2 and played in the Big 12 Tournament championship vs Houston
Arkansas won the SEC Tournament but their defensive weakness (102.1 AdjD) is a critical vulnerability against Arizona's elite offense
Arizona's 31-2 record and Big 12 dominance reflect one of the most complete teams in the country

Submit your updates here. › Arizona forward Koa Peat made his return in an emphatic way as the Wildcats cruised to an 84-61 win over the Kansas Jayhawks at home. Peat had missed Arizona's previous three games after suffering a lower body injury against Texas Tech on Feb. 14, but his return has raised the ceiling for one of the best teams in the country heading into the NCAA Tournament. Why it matters Peat's performance against Kansas is a positive sign for Arizona as they look to make a deep run in the NCAA Tournament. The Wildcats were able to survive his absence, but having Peat back at full strength makes them an even more dangerous team. The details Peat scored 12 points on 4-9 shooting, grabbed 7 rebounds, and dished out 5 assists in 23 minutes of action against Kansas. Head coach Tommy Lloyd spoke about the impact of having Peat back on the court, saying it's 'good to kind of get all the pieces back together' as they look to 'reform into something even better down the stretch of the season'. Peat suffered a lower body injury against Texas Tech on Feb. 14. Peat missed Arizona's previous three games before returning against Kansas on Feb. 28, 2026. Arizona has two regular season games remaining against Iowa State and Colorado. Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize the data:

**Arizona (1-seed):**
- KenPom #3: AdjO 127.2 (7th), AdjD 89.7 (best in country), AdjEM +37.56
- Torvik #3: AdjO 126.2 (10th), AdjD 91.1 (3rd), Record 31-2
- Koa Peat returned healthy from leg injury on March 2, played in Big 12 Tournament championship vs Houston
- Elite two-way team, dominant defense

**Arkansas (4-seed):**
- KenPom #17: AdjO 127.6 (5th), AdjD 102.1, AdjEM +25.43
- Torvik #20: AdjO 127.8 (5th), AdjD 102.0 (50th), Record 25-8
- SEC Tournament champions under Calipari, strong momentum
- Elite offense (5th nationally) but mediocre defense (50th)

**Key matchup dynamics:**
- Efficiency margin gap: +37.56 vs +25.43 = ~12 points per 100 possessions
- Arizona's defense (89.7) vs Arkansas's offense (127.6) is THE critical matchup
- Arkansas has the 5th-best offense nationally, but Arizona has the best defense - this is an irresistible force vs immovable object scenario
- Arizona's offense (127.2) vs Arkansas's defense (102.1) is a massive mismatch favoring Arizona
- Both teams healthy, both with momentum (Arizona 31-2, Arkansas SEC champs)

The ~12-point efficiency margin gap is enormous for a 1-vs-4 matchup. Arkansas's elite offense is their best weapon, but Arizona's historically elite defense should significantly limit it. Meanwhile, Arizona's 7th-ranked offense should feast on Arkansas's 50th-ranked defense.
